text: Most of the commercial solvent extractants in use for extractive hydrometallurgy act in various modes of action, according to the process route of the recovery of the metal which involves the transport to the organic phase of M(n+), or metalate species MXx(n-), or metal salts MXx. The factors responsible for the coordinative bonding which lead to the known strengths and selectivities of most extractants, including synergistic pairs, are generally based on well-established principles of coordination chemistry. Reagents which impart selectivity for transport of M(n+) or MXx to the organic phase in most cases operate in the inner coordination sphere of the metal ion and provide a suitable combination of suitable ligand atom types in an appropriate disposition allowing the formation of relatively stable neutral species which are soluble in hydrocarbons. When it is the transport of metalate species which is required, the neutral complex species in the organic phase are often poorly characterized and the cationic reagents are thought to act mainly in the outer coordination sphere by utilizing their ability to form secondary bonds, often by electrostatic as well as by H-bonding in particular because of the low dielectric constant of the organic phases involved.

Reagents which impart selectivity for transport of M(n+) or MXx to the organic phase in most cases operate in the inner coordination sphere of the metal ion and provide a suitable combination of suitable ligand atom types in an appropriate disposition allowing the formation of relatively stable neutral species which are soluble in hydrocarbons.
